title: Clearing an airlock from a cold tap
subtitle: Push trapped air out of a low-pressure cold supply by back-feeding from a high-pressure cold tap
excerpt: How to clear the spluttering, low flow that follows a drain-down or a stopcock work. Covers identifying an airlock, the hose-link method between a high-pressure and a low-pressure cold tap, and following the air through the pipework until it clears.

glossaryTerms.definition: A bubble of trapped air in a plumbing run that blocks water flow. Common after a drain-down or stopcock work. Shows as low flow, spluttering, or no flow at all on the affected tap.
Connecting a high-pressure tap to a low-pressure tap with a short hose so that mains pressure pushes the airlock back up to the cold-water tank, where it escapes.

aliases: clearing trapped air from a tap

content.text: An
airlock

sits in a cold-water pipe when the supply has been drained and refilled. The air bubble blocks gravity flow from a tank-fed tap. The fix is to push the air back out, using mains pressure from another tap.
When to use this technique

Use this method when one cold tap runs weak or spluttering after a drain-down, a new tap fit, or a stopcock isolation. The trick only works on a tank-fed cold tap: the high-pressure supply (mains-fed kitchen cold) pushes air back up to the cold tank in the loft. Mains-fed taps almost never airlock; the pressure clears small bubbles by itself.
What you need

A 600 mm length of garden hose with a tap connector at each end. The connectors must fit your tap thread. Most kitchen taps and bath cold taps take a standard 22 mm threaded connector with a rubber washer. A hose with a snap-fit female end on each side is the easiest.
Step 1, identify the source and the weak tap

The source tap is a high-pressure cold tap that flows normally. The kitchen cold tap is almost always mains-fed and is the standard source. The weak tap is the low-flow tap. Confirm the affected tap is tank-fed by tracing the pipe: a 22 mm cold pipe that comes from the loft is tank-fed; a 15 mm cold pipe under the kitchen sink is mains-fed.
Step 2, link the two taps with the hose

Fit one end of the hose to the source tap (kitchen cold) and the other to the weak tap (the blocked one). Open both taps fully. The mains pressure from the source pushes water back through the weak tap, against the normal flow, into the tank-fed run. The trapped air gets driven back up the pipe to the cold tank, where it escapes through the ball valve.
Step 3, listen for the air to clear

Hold the hose link for 30 seconds to a minute. The blocked pipe will gurgle as the air shifts. When the gurgle stops and the flow back through the source feels steady, close the weak tap first, then the source tap. Disconnect the hose. Open the weak tap normally. It should flow at full pressure. This
back-feeding
method is the standard plumber's fix.
What it looks like when it is right
The weak tap runs steady and silent within a minute. A second slow run of any other tank-fed tap shows full flow as well; clearing one airlock often clears a related one.
Common mistakes

Hose link on the wrong taps. A mains-to-mains link does nothing; both sides are already at mains pressure. A tank-to-tank link does nothing; neither side has the head to push the air. The link must run mains to tank.
Closing the source tap before the weak tap. The mains pressure drops and the weak tap sucks back air through the hose, undoing the work. Always close the weak tap first, then the source.
A small pinhole in the hose. A leak in the hose mid-run lets air in and stops the airlock clearing. Inspect the hose before fitting; replace any hose with cracks or perished sections.
